--- SOURCE 1: Simplifying microbial electrosynthesis reactor design - PMC ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4432714/

SUMMARY:
In traditional ‘H-cell’ reactors, where the anode and cathode chambers were separated with a proton-selective membrane, the rates and columbic efficiencies of microbial electrosynthesis remained high when electron delivery at the cathode was powered with a direct current power source rather than with a potentiostat-poised cathode utilized in previous studies. Unmodified reactors were run as controls to compare to the reactors studied with poised cathode potentials and were constructed as described in Section “Culturing Techniques in Microbial Electrosynthesis Reactors.” Additionally, two H-cells were run with an increased applied voltage over the system to boost current density and to encourage acetate production (Table 1). In order to determine if even higher applied voltages would be effective in yielding greater rates of acetate production, microbial electrosynthesis in an H-cell reactor with an applied voltage of 5.0 V was evaluated.

--- SOURCE 3: Improved reactor design enables productivity of microbial ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378

SUMMARY:
In sum, acetate production rates in the described MES plate reactor far exceeded published rates of acetate electrosynthesis of <0.78 g L−1 catholyte h−1 (LaBelle et al., 2020) and even surpassed 50-day sustained rates of mixed acid electrosynthesis reported recently (1.8 g C L−1 cathode h−1 which would correspond to 4.5 g L−1 cathode h−1 and 0.3 g L−1 catholyte h−1 pure acetate) (Cabau-Peinado et al., 2024). However, in line with the comparison to the glucose-fed chemostat, at comparable acetate productivity of 3–3.5 g L−1 h−1 (∼60% hydrogen utilization), hydrogen utilization during MES was similar (64%, 60ppiKiv2) or more efficient (88–92% 60ppiKiv1) compared to gas fermentation (Kantzow et al., 2015) indicating superior hydrogen supply efficiency by the plate reactor (Fig. 5B).

Improved reactor design enables productivity of microbial electrosynthesis on par with classical biotechnology
==============================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Jörg S.Deutzmann a c, Grace Callander b c, Alfred M.Spormann a b c

Add to Mendeley

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2024.131733](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2024.131733 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0960852424014378&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Plate reactor design enables high-rate microbial electrosynthesis (MES). 
*   •High-rate electromethanogenesis obtained by _M. maripaludis_ and mixed culture. 
*   •MES of acetate by _T. kivui_ on par with glucose fermentation rates. 
*   •Acetate production rates by MES approach commercial gas fermentation rates. 
*   •Improved electron donor utilization by MES compared to classical biotechnology. 

Abstract
--------

Microbial electrosynthesis (MES) converts (renewable) electrical energy into CO 2-derived chemicals including fuels. To achieve commercial viability of this process, improvements in production rate, energy efficiency, and product titer are imperative. Employing a compact plate reactor with zero gap anode configuration and NiMo-plated reticulated vitreous carbon cathodes substantially improved electrosynthesis rates of methane and acetic acid. Electromethanogenesis rates exceeded 10 L L–1 catholyte d–1 using an undefined mixed culture. Continuous thermophilic MES by _Thermoanaerobacter kivui_ produced acetic acid at a rate of up to 3.5 g L−1 catholyte h−1 at a titer of 14 g/L _,_ surpassing continuous gas fermentation without biomass retention and on par with glucose fermentation by _T. kivui_ in chemostats. Coulombic efficiencies reached 80%–90% and energy efficiencies up to 30% for acetate and methane production. The performance of this plate reactor demonstrates that MES can deliver production rates that are competitive with those of established biotechnologies.

1. Introduction
---------------

Microbial electrosynthesis (MES) is emerging as a promising platform biotechnology that uses (renewable) electricity to reduce CO 2 to value-added chemicals, including fuels. Thereby, MES represents a path to produce carbon–neutral compounds for a circular carbon economy. MES leverages electricity-powered electrochemical reactions to drive microbial metabolism and the selective formation of CO 2-derived products. Methane and acetic acid are among the thermodynamically and kinetically most favorable biomolecules that can be produced via MES ([Dessì et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0045)). Microorganisms, methanogens or acetogens, reduce CO 2 to methane or acetate, respectively, with cathode-derived electrons or with _in-situ_ produced H 2. Each are single catabolic products of specialized microbial metabolisms, which provide limited energy for microbial growth; thus, limiting the amount of biomass produced.

While hydrogenotrophic methanogens are mostly limited to H 2+CO 2 or formic acid as substrates, acetogens, such as the thermophilic _Thermoanaerobacter kivui_ or the mesophilic _Acetobacterium woodii,_ also produce acetic acid from other substrates, such as carbohydrates. Industrial acetic acid production by acetogens from carbohydrates was reviewed three decades ago ([Wiegel, 1994](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0170)) but has not been successfully adopted commercially. In these anaerobic fermentations, glucose, usually a non-limiting substrate due to its high solubility and low toxicity, is metabolized by acetogens to three acetate molecules; two are produced directly from glucose and one via H 2+CO 2. Thus, acetogenic bacteria use the same pathway in gas fermentation as they do for the production of a third of the acetate during glucose fermentation, but the latter allows a higher growth yield and denser cultures due to a higher ATP yield ([Katsyv et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0100)).

MES is an integrated technology of an otherwise two-step process: 1) Chemical water or CO 2 electrolysis to produce H 2, CO, or formic acid in an electrochemical cell and 2) introduction of these products as substrates to a separate microbial bioreactor. Fermentation of exogenously-fed gases H 2, CO 2, and CO (gas fermentation) has already been scaled up and approaches economic feasibility to produce biomethane, bioethanol, or other biochemicals. Continuous gas fermentation of H 2+CO 2 to acetic acid has achieved a maximum production rate of 0.75 g L−1 h−1 at a titer of 27 g/L, and with full cell retention even 6.16 g L−1 h−1 at 17.6 g/L using _Acetobacterium woodii_ ([Kantzow et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0095)). Ethanol production rate optimized by Lanzatech is estimated to be 10 g L−1 h−1 ([Takors et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0160)). H 2+CO 2 conversion to methane was achieved at an impressive rate of 33 L L−1 h−1 using thermophilic methanogens in pressurized gas fermentation reactors ([Electrochaea, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0060)).

In just more than a decade, MES has evolved from a laboratory concept to a nascent technology whose economic viability is debated ([Bajracharya et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0020), [Izadi and Harnisch, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0085), [Jourdin and Burdyny, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0090), [Prévoteau et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0145)). One limiting factor of MES is that volumetric productivities have remained substantially lower than gas fermentation, although similar microbes are used in both processes. MES of acetate has reached at a maximum rate of 0.78 g L−1 catholyte h−1 (at a titer of 3.6 g/L) in a mixed culture plate reactor system ([LaBelle and May 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0125)), or 0.76 g L−1 catholyte h−1 (at a titer of 23 g/L) using a pure culture of _T. kivui_ in an H-cell type reactor ([Deutzmann and Spormann, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0055)). MES of methane, or microbial electromethanogenesis (MEM), has been reported with a maximum rate of 0.52 L−1 cathode h−1 ([Geppert et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0070)). Thus, significant improvements in MES productivity are needed to compete with gas fermentation.

Productivities in gas fermentation are typically limited by mass transfer of the gas to the liquid ([Ale Enriquez and Ahring, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0005)). MES aims to overcome this mass transfer limitation by forming gas at the cathode (preferentially at the solubility limit) in the bioreactor, thereby making the gases accessible to microbes without the need for engineered mass transfer. However, generally low MES productivity indicates that efficient delivery of electron donors has also been limiting MES ([Jourdin and Burdyny, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0090), [Prévoteau et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0145)). Therefore, design of efficient (bio)cathodes and electrochemical reactors is needed to increase key performance parameters of MES. One prerequisite for high production rates is high surface area cathodes to deliver hydrogen to the microbes efficiently. Indeed, most studies reporting high MES rates have used high surface area electrodes ([Flexer and Jourdin, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0065), [Jourdin and Burdyny, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0090)). To enhance the energy efficiency of MES, the most important reactor design parameter is a short distance between the anode and cathode, which minimizes the resistance due to the modest conductivity of microbial media ([Prévoteau et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0145)). Therefore, flat plate reactors with narrow anode and cathode compartments, preferentially with a zero gap between anode and membrane, are attractive for MES. Further, these reactors can theoretically be stacked, resembling the design of other and established electrochemical platforms, such as electrolyzers and fuel cells.

The compact plate reactor design with a zero-gap anode and porous reticulated vitreous carbon (RVC)-based flow-through cathode presented here demonstrates exceptional performance of both methane and acetate production. In this reactor, MES of acetate by a pure culture of _T.kivui_ approached volumetric productivity similar to gas or glucose fermentation, indicating equivalent electron donor supply rates. This work demonstrates that MES has the potential to produce reduced carbon compounds at competing rates to classical biotechnology platforms.

2. Material and methods
-----------------------

### 2.1. Microbial cultures and media

_Thermoanaerobacter kivui_ DSM 2030 was pre-cultured in serum bottles and cultivated in the electrochemical cells in mineral medium as described previously ([Deutzmann and Spormann, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0055)). _Methanococcus maripaludis_ was cultivated in serum bottles and the electrochemical cells as described previously ([Deutzmann et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0050)). The mixed methanogenic culture was obtained by inoculating the previously pure culture _M.maripaludis_ electrochemical cell with 100 µL of aged digestate from a cow manure-fed thermophilic anaerobic digester. For details on medium composition and cultivation conditions, see additional data.

### 2.2. Plate reactor: design and assembly

In short, the electrochemical reactor consisted of a cathode compartment filled with a porous reticulated vitreous carbon (RVC) electrode compressed onto a proton exchange membrane and a porous anode. The 9 cm diameter circular cathode compartment of ¼ inch (6.35 mm) thickness was filled completely with a 30, 60, or 100 ppi (pores per inch, ∼11.8, 23.6, or 39.4 pores per cm) NiMo-coated RVC electrode (ERG) resulting in geometric cathode surface areas of about 688, 1458, and 2673 cm 2, calculated from the cathode volume and the manufacturer’s data on the specific surface area of Duocel® foam. The RVC electrode was electroplated with NiMo alloy to facilitate hydrogen evolution (2 A current, until the surface appeared uniformly silver). Further, a serpentine flow path was generated by inserting graphite foil strips (0.254 mm thickness) into razor-blade-cuts in the RVC electrode. The length of the strips was 1 cm shorter than the width of the electrode at that location ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#f0005)C+D). The cathode was connected to a titanium current collector (sheet of 0.007 in. (0.178 mm) shim stock, Trinity Brand Industries) through a layer of plain carbon cloth (CCP40, fuel Cell Earth) and a graphite foil sheet (0.254 mm thickness) (order: RVC-cloth-graphite-Ti). The internal volume of the cathode compartment was 40 mL. The medium was recirculated through the cathode using a 12 V DC water pump (JT-160, max pump rate 4.7 L/min, pump rate through freshly assembled reactors: 100–300 mL/min, max pump pressure ∼0.23 bar) in a recirculation loop such that the total volume of the catholyte including the recirculation loop was 48 mL (reactor 60ppiKiv2) to 64 mL due to different sizes of tubing, pH electrode holders, and Hungate tubes (reactors 60ppiKiv2, 100ppiMM, 60ppiMix, see 2.3). The volume of the catholyte was determined by carefully filling the assembled system with water and quantifying the volume. The actual volume during operation was likely lower due to biomass accumulation and trapped gas in the pores of the cathode. The cathode compartment was separated from the anode compartment by a cation exchange membrane (Nafion 117 or fumasep FKB-PK-130). The anode consisted of a Pt-coated sintered titanium disk or platinized titanium fiber felt pressed against the membrane in a zero-gap electrode configuration ([Baek et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0015), [Bian et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0030)). The area of the membrane and projected electrode areas were 64 cm 2. The end plate on the cathode side consisted of stainless steel for all _T. kivui_ reactors and the 60ppiMix reactor or polycarbonate for the 100ppiMM reactor. All anode-side end plates also consisted of polycarbonate. The stack was closed by tightening six screws until all components were firmly pressed together. Heating to 65°C was achieved by adhesive polyimide heating strips (Icstation) controlled by thermostats (W1209, Amazon) with the temperature probe embedded in the recirculation pump tube stopper. For details on each reactor configuration and a list of parts, see additional data.

![Image 12](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0960852424014378-gr1.jpg)

2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0960852424014378-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Plate reactor design. A) Expanded view of an exemplary circular plate reactor. Parts from left to right: Stainless steel endplate with Epichlorohydrin Foam O-ring, ¼ inch (6.35 mm) RVC cathode in a ¼ inch (6.35 mm) cPVC ring, O-ring, proton exchange membrane, O-ring, Pt-coated sintered titanium disk anode with spacers towards the endplate-side, O-ring, polycarbonate endplate. For more information, see additional data. B) Photograph of the assembled plate reactor with medium recirculation loop. For more information, see supplementary information. C) Photograph of the 60ppiMix RVC cathode with serpentine flow field before the experiment. D) Photograph of the same RVC cathode after the 60ppiMix reactor experiment. The beige color of extensive biomass accumulation on the RVC pore space is clearly visible.

### 2.3. Plate reactor: operation

The cathode compartment of the electrochemical reactor was filled with anoxic medium by a peristaltic pump. The catholyte was recirculated through the cathode compartment via an external recirculation loop. The recirculation loop contained a pump, a glass Hungate tube for sampling and visual access of the medium, a connection for gas supply, the temperature probe, and, in case of acetogenic operation, a pH probe. Medium input, base addition, and medium outlet were also connected via the top stopper of the tube in the recirculation loop (see [Supplementary information](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#s0085)). The dilution rate of the growth medium was between 1 d−1 and 6.2 d−1 as indicated. Reactors were inoculated with 12 mL of actively growing H 2+CO 2-fed cultures of OD between 0.1 and 0.3. In most experiments, current density, CO 2 supply, and dilution rate were adjusted incrementally to test impacts on MES productivity. CO 2 was supplied as N 2/CO 2 (80/20) gas mix in experiments 30ppiKiv, 100ppiMM, and part of 60ppiKiv1 to ensure sufficient gas flow for the gas measurement system. Later, pure CO 2 was used (60ppiKiv2, 60ppiMix, and end of 60ppiKiv1) because gas outflow (unused H 2+CO 2) was sufficient for automated gas analysis at higher currents. Gas flow rates were adjusted regularly and resulted in the CO 2 supply data plotted in the results section for each experiment. After the upper acetate concentration, which limits acetate MES, was established (see below), acetate concentration was kept below 20 g/L in 60ppiKiv1+2. The reactor outlets were open to the air (anode side) or connected to about+20 mbar of water column in a gas–liquid separation bottle on the cathode side. Therefore, pressure usually dropped from less than 0.23 bar overpressure generated by the recirculation pump to close to atmospheric pressure at the outlets. Anode compartments were either flushed from the top connection to the outlet at the bottom by a CO 2 or N 2/CO 2 gas stream with 1–3 mL/min to avoid flooding of the anode compartments and to allow gas analysis of the anodic gas outflow, or simply plugged at the top outlet and the accumulating oxygen was pushed out of the bottom outlet with traces of accumulating liquid (see additional data for more details on reactor assembly and operation).

### 2.4. Analytical methods

Acetic acid concentration was determined from 0.5 mL samples via high-performance liquid chromatography on an Aminex HPX-87H column (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA) with 5 mM H 2 SO 4 as the eluent, at a flow rate of 0.7 mL/min. Other common fermentation products, including formic acid, ethanol, butyrate, butanol, and lactate that can be quantified with the same method were not detected in significant concentrations (<1 mM).

Acetic acid production rates were calculated by multiplying the acetic acid concentration with the medium outflow rate. Medium outflow was measured with a graduated cylinder over 2–5 h. Due to the frequent intentionally-induced alterations to the reactors, acetate production rates are only reported for periods of steady production. Further, reported acetate productivities were averaged over at least three consecutive measurements over at least three days.

Methane and hydrogen were quantified via gas chromatography (GC, Multiple Gas Analyzer #3 GC 8610–0371 − 8610C, SRI Instruments) using a 6′ Hayesep D column (SRI Instruments). The GC was operated isothermally at 50°C with 12 PSI N 2 (NF, Praxair) as carrier gas. Sampling was performed by manual injection of a 300 µL sample using a gas-tight syringe (VICI) or by automated measurements as described previously ([Deutzmann et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0050)).

Gas flow rates were determined using a MilliGascounter (Ritter, Germany). The gas counter was connected to the outlet of the cathode compartment behind a gas–liquid separation bottle to prevent liquid from the MES outflow from contaminating the counter. Gas flow measurements were timed in between automated GC samplings to prevent any impact on these measurements.

Methane production rates (MPRs) were calculated using two methods. Minimum MPRs were calculated directly from infrequently measured gas flow rates from the cathode compartment of the MES system and the fraction of methane in the gas stream as determined by GC. MPRs presented prominently and used for comparisons to other studies are based on these gas flow and concentration measurements. They are referred to as “measured” throughout the manuscript to contrast them to the “inferred” rates which are mainly used to visualize the temporal changes in MPR. MPRs were inferred from the automated gas analyses assuming stoichiometric conversion of hydrogen to methane according to Eq. [(1)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#e0005). Using the ratio of methane to hydrogen in the off-gas and the hydrogen formation rate calculated from the supplied current (HFR, Eq. [(2)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#e0010)) yielded the equation for the MPR (Eq. [(3)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#e0015)). Inferred MPRs overestimate actual methane production, because losses due to H 2 and Methane diffusion across the membrane and biomass formation are not considered.(1)4 H 2+CO 2→CH 4+2 H 2 O(2)HFR=I·RT nFV·3600·24(3)MPR=H F R·pC H 4 4·p C H 4+p H 2

With _HFR_ [L L−1 d−1]: hydrogen formation rate, _I_ [A]: current, _R_ [L bar K−1 mol−1]: ideal gas constant=0.0831446, _T_ [K] temperature=303, _n_=2 for two electrons required to form H 2, _F_ [s A/mol]: Faraday constant=96485.3321, _V_ [L]: Volume of MES catholyte, _MFR_ [L L−1 d−1]: methane formation rate, _pH_ _2_: partial pressure of hydrogen, _pCH_ _4_: partial pressure of CH 4 in the off-gas. 3600 and 24 are the factors used to convert seconds to hours and days, respectively.

Electricity-powered hydrogen formation on NiMo-plated electrodes has been shown to be almost 100% efficient ([Kracke et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0115)), and 100% efficiency is assumed for this calculation. Likewise, methanogenesis from H 2+CO 2 has been shown to be more than 90% efficient, as less than 10% of the substrates, usually significantly less under non-ideal growth conditions, are used for cell mass production ([Kracke et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0115)).

Coulombic and Energy efficiency were calculated according to the following formulas:(4)CE product=n product∙z product∙F∫I t∙d t∙100%where _CE_ _product_ is the Coulombic efficiency for a desired product (%), _n_ _product_ is the amount of product (mol), _z_ _product_ is the number of electrons per mol product formation, _F_ is the Faraday constant (96.485 C/mol), and _I_ is the current (A).(5)EE product=E eq E cell∙CE product where _EE_ _product_ is the energy efficiency for product formation (%), _E_ _eq_ is the equilibrium voltage needed for product formation (1.06 V for methane plus oxygen and 1.11 V for acetic acid plus oxygen), _E_ _cell_ is the voltage applied to the electrochemical cell, and _CE_ _product_ is the Coulombic efficiency (Eq. [(4)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#e0020)).

3. Results and discussion
-------------------------

### 3.1. Plate reactor: design and performance

The circular plate reactor ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#f0005)) was designed and implemented based on insights into fundamental limitations obtained from MES studies using H-cell reactors ([Deutzmann and Spormann 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0055)). Notably, the cathode compartment thickness of ¼ in (6.35 mm) reduced the internal resistance significantly compared to H-cell reactors used for MES of acetate with _T. kivui_ and the same medium ([Deutzmann and Spormann, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0960852424014378#b0055)). While the H-cell system was unable to sustain more than 2.85 A/L catholyte volum

The aim of the project is to develop a high-efficiency reactor concept that enables high acetate production rates and yields by enhancing biomass growth and

FULL CONTENT:
# Optimizing microbial electrosynthesis: from microbial communities to reactor design

![](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fcontent-webapi.tuni.fi%2Fimage-style%2Fw1440_h480_manual_crop%2Fproxy%2Fpublic%2F2021-03%2Fnrichie_kuva.jpg%3Fitok%3DShpwN0PQ&w=3840&q=75)

In this project, microbial conversion of carbon dioxide rich gases to organic acids, building blocks for example for bioplastics and biodiesel, is optimized in a process called microbial electrosynthesis. This project promotes circular economy that requires novel processes to upgrade materials to higher value products. In addition, the biological process enables the sequestration of greenhouse gas, carbon dioxide. The aim of the project is to develop a high-efficiency reactor concept that enables high acetate production rates and yields by enhancing biomass growth and mass transfer. Furthermore, the process will be optimized based on the results of microbial community analyses.

## Funding source

Academy of Finland

enriched_points.expanded_content: Improved reactor design enhances electron delivery and biomass retention, increasing microbial electrosynthesis rates and efficiencies, as seen in plate reactors with high surface area cathodes.
Utilizing renewable energy sources like solar or wind can power microbial electrosynthesis, reducing carbon footprints and supporting sustainable production of biofuels and chemicals.
Identifying and utilizing novel microbial strains can optimize product yields and enhance metabolic pathways, leading to more efficient CO2 conversion into valuable compounds.
